title: Introduce GPU ephemeral backup tfmodule and tfstate anchoring helper
preview:
Background and motivation:
This PR introduces two small, composable terraform modules to reduce operational risk during Private installations that use GPU node pools and sharded tfstate layouts. We've seen recurring incidents where control-plane drift or partial apply failures during nodepool upgrades required manual state surgeries (ENG-4821). The goal is to provide a repeatable, automated safety net: ephemeral backups of nodepool metadata and a lightweight tfstate anchoring helper that produces a signed checkpoint file distributed to the ops bucket.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup: an opt-in module that creates a snapshot of the nodepool control metadata, saves kubelet/CRI config snippets, and records a lightweight manifest (node labels, taints, instance-ids) to the configured artifacts bucket. Designed for very short retention (24–72h) to minimize storage and compliance exposure.
- Adds modules/tfstate_anchor: helper that, when invoked, creates a signed checkpoint object (HMAC using a KMS key) that includes the backend state version and a checksum of critical tf resources; intended as a checkpoint for emergency rollbacks and as a hook for the tfstate-migrator workflow.
- Wire-up examples in examples/private/gpu-upgrade-safety which demonstrate using both modules around a nodepool upgrade plan (pre-apply snapshot, post-apply anchor).
- Tests and CI: adds terratest-style integration snippets and unit validation via tflint/tfsec CI gates.

Why this is different from existing state helpers: 
- Lightweight: we do not duplicate full tfstate snapshots (those are expensive/slow). Instead we capture a minimal, verifiable checkpoint that is sufficient to detect drift and reconstruct resource pointers for the tfstate reconciler.
- Kubernetes-aware: the backup manifest captures node-level Kubernetes metadata necessary to reattach pods/stateful-resources to replacement nodes.
- Designed for Private: integrates with our private artifacts bucket patterns, KMS key usage, and tenancy-scoped IAM roles.

Files changed (high-level):
- modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup/main.tf (new)
- modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup/outputs.tf (new)
- modules/gpu_ephemeral_backup/variables.tf (new)
- modules/tfstate_anchor/main.tf (new)
- modules/tfstate_anchor/README.md (new)
- examples/private/gpu-upgrade-safety/README.md (new)
- examples/private/gpu-upgrade-safety/main.tf (new)
- ci/terraform/lint.yml (updated to run tflint on new modules)
- tests/integration/gpu_ephemeral_backup_test.go (new terratest stub)

Commits (high-level):
1) init: add gpu_ephemeral_backup module skeleton and basic manifest writer
2) anchor: implement tfstate_anchor with KMS-signed checkpoint generation and helper outputs
3) examples & docs: add example deployment and README guidance for upgrade hooks
4) ci & tests: add tflint/tfsec CI changes and a terratest stub; fix lint issues raised during CI

Design notes and implementation details:
- The ephemeral backup module is intentionally small: it runs a single provider-backed data collection step that queries cloud provider instance metadata and the kube API (via optional provider credentials) and marshals a manifest object to an artifacts bucket. It exposes a short-lived pre-signed URL and a retention TTL.
- The tfstate_anchor module computes a canonical digest (sorted keys) of the supplied critical resources (map input) and writes a JSON checkpoint to the artifacts bucket. A KMS HMAC key (or HSM-backed symmetric key) is used to sign the payload; signature and signer metadata are included in outputs. Consumers (tfstate-migrator, upgrade-runner) can validate anchors before performing destructive operations.
- Both modules are usage-optional and can be wired into existing plan/apply pipelines in private deployments. We do not alter any existing core tfstate backends by default.

Security and compliance considerations:
- Checkpoints contain metadata but do NOT include full credentials or secrets. Any optional kube API access is performed via the provided role/credentials; default behavior is metadata-only.
- Artifacts bucket ACLs follow existing private deploy patterns; the module requires a kms_key resource ARN and a role that can sign/verify.
- Retention defaults to 24 hours but is configurable to meet customer compliance needs.

Testing and validation performed:
- Local terratest run against a small dev project (mocked provider) validated manifest shape and signature validation flow.
- CI added tflint rules and tfsec suppressions with justifications in README.
- Manual validation with the tfstate-migrator dev harness: created anchor -> deliberately induced partial apply -> run migrator validation -> verified anchor prevented unsafe rollback without explicit acknowledge.

Migration/upgrade path and rollback plan:
- This is opt-in. Existing Private installs will not be affected unless the module is included in their private deployment stacks.
- Recommended rollout: include gpu_ephemeral_backup and tfstate_anchor in canary tenant stacks for one week; validate creation of artifacts and signature verification; then expand to remaining tenants.
- In case of problems the modules can be removed without impacting core state backends; artifacts are purely supplemental.

Review thread (summary):
- Ava Chen: asked for stronger validation on the manifest schema and a unit test for missing fields; requested the retention default be 24h instead of 7d for security.
- Author reply: added a JSON schema validation, added test cases, and updated default retention to 24h (see commit 3).
- Diego Morales: asked for explicit IAM permission docs and a suggestion to avoid broad storage ACLs.
- Author reply: added IAM role policy snippet in README and switched to bucket-level objects with signer-only put permissions.
- Liam O'Connor: ran a small integration using the private installer dev cluster and requested a minor rename of output keys for consistency; author updated outputs in commit 4.
- Final: reviewers approved and requested small followup to add production terratest scenarios later.

CI history and resolution:
- Initial pipeline: tflint reported two stylistic issues and tfsec flagged an allowed IAM pattern; CI failed on first run.
- Fixes: corrected tflint warnings, added tfsec suppressions with rationale, updated lint workflow. Final CI green on run #3.

Operational notes for SREs/Field engineers:
- When enabling the modules in a tenant, ensure the artifacts bucket uses lifecycle rules and that KMS keys are rotated per org policy.
- The anchor verification command (scripts/verify_anchor.sh) is included in the examples directory; use it to validate anchors before applying rollbacks.

Follow-ups (future work):
- Add a small operator runbook to automatically reconcile artifacts cleanup post-upgrade.
- Integrate anchor validation as a pre-check in the private-installer upgrade controller.
- Expand terratest coverage to a production-like environment and add a scheduled job that validates anchor creation.

TL;DR: Adds two opt-in terraform helpers to make GPU nodepool upgrades and tfstate-boundary migrations safer by providing short-lived backups and cryptographically-signed checkpoint anchors. Opt-in, backwards-compatible, and CI-tested.

title: Marketplace provisioning: IAM + webhook config for entitlement sync (least privilege)
preview: Context: Marketplace-driven customers need a reliable subscription → entitlement → org provisioning flow. The control-plane entitlement sync service now consumes entitlement signals from both AWS Marketplace (API polling + optional notifications) and GCP Marketplace (vendor webhook). This PR adds Terraform-managed IAM, network, and webhook plumbing needed by the entitlement sync services while keeping permissions least-privilege and auditable. Changes: (1) AWS IAM: new dedicated IAM role for entitlement-sync with scoped permissions to aws-marketplace entitlement + metering APIs; separate role for webhook verifier to read secrets and write limited logs/metrics; explicit deny for broad S3 and KMS actions; (2) Webhook ingress: add an internal ALB listener rule + path-based routing for /marketplace/gcp/webhook to the entitlement-sync service target group; add WAF rule set and request size limits; (3) Secrets and key usage: provision an SSM parameter + Secrets Manager secret for GCP webhook signing key rotation, and KMS grants scoped to decrypt only those secrets; (4) Observability + safety: cloudwatch alarms for 5xx spikes on the webhook path, and metric filters for signature verification failures; (5) Documentation: inline least-privilege notes and a short README section on how to validate in staging. Notes: This does not change application logic—only infra wiring and policy boundaries. Follow-up: once we confirm partner testing, we can enable optional SNS-based AWS notifications in prod (module is included but off by default).

title: perf-canary: wire service account, secret storage, and least-privilege IAM
preview:
Summary: This PR adds the Terraform wiring needed to deploy perf-canary safely: (1) a dedicated service account with least-privilege IAM, (2) secret storage + access policies for canary auth, and (3) environment/region guardrails to prevent accidental fanout. This unblocks the perf-canary Helm chart rollout by providing a constrained identity that can call internal inference endpoints and emit metrics without broad access.

Context: perf-canary runs synthetic workloads against internal inference endpoints and publishes performance metrics/alerts. We need a dedicated identity for the canary deployment (no shared runtime/telemetry SA), scoped to: read-only inference invoke, write-only metrics, and read-only baseline artifact access. We also need to store the canary auth token / client secret in managed secret storage and allow only the canary workload identity to decrypt/access it.

What changed (high level): - Introduced perf_canary_service_account module (GCP) and associated IAM bindings. - Added Secret Manager secrets for perf-canary auth material and a KMS policy to allow decrypt only by the canary SA. - Added explicit IAM conditions and narrowed resource scopes (project/namespace/cluster) to avoid granting broad viewer/editor roles. - Added a small set of env/region toggles (enable_perf_canary, perf_canary_regions allowlist) and validation to avoid enabling canary in all regions by mistake.

IAM scope (intended): - Inference invoke: allow calling internal inference gateway (limited audience/scope; no general service-to-service). - Metrics: allow write to telemetry ingest (no read, no admin). - Baselines: allow read from baseline bucket/prefix only (no list on entire bucket, no write). - Secrets: allow access to perf-canary secret versions only (no create/delete; no wildcard).

Operational notes: - Secret rotation: this PR creates the secret resources and IAM; rotation procedures remain in the infra runbook. - Rollback: disabling perf-canary is done by setting enable_perf_canary=false in the environment tfvars; IAM resources remain but are inert.

Testing/verification: - terraform validate + fmt - terraform plan for staging-us-east and prod-us-east: no unexpected diffs outside perf-canary resources - Manual check: SA cannot list other secrets or buckets; can read only the one secret and baseline prefix.

Risk: Medium. IAM changes are production-impacting; however this PR only adds new bindings/resources (no removal of existing roles). Reviewers: please sanity-check IAM conditions and secret access boundaries.

Follow-ups: - Consider adding org-policy constraint to block secret access from non-approved service accounts (separate PR) - If we move baselines from GCS to config service, we should replace the bucket read binding accordingly.

Refs: Linear ENG-4879 (Deployment helm + terraform), ENG-4872 (MVP), ENG-4878 (alerting routing). Jira INT-2213 (service account permissions request).

title: DNS TTL tuning + routing primitives for faster regional failover
preview:
Summary: This PR updates Terraform for Redwood’s cross-region traffic steering so regional failover can converge faster and more predictably during impairment. The main change is lowering DNS TTLs for the API entrypoints used by Hosted API and Dedicated (where applicable), while adding guardrails to avoid unintended traffic shifts (provider caching variance, resolver behavior, and over-eager health checks). It also adds explicit routing primitives (health checks + failover policy wiring + outputs for the failover controller) so the automation controller can rely on consistent infra definitions across environments.

Motivation / context: Regional failover automation upgrades (Reliability Program) require that DNS-based steering not be the long pole. Today, several records use TTLs that are appropriate for steady-state but extend failover time, and some resources are defined inconsistently across regions/environments. We want a standard, reviewable baseline that aligns with the failover signal hierarchy and avoids oscillation during partial outages.

What changed: 1) TTL tuning: Reduce TTLs for public API endpoints (api.redwoodinference.com + region aliases) from 60s -> 20s in prod (staging remains 10s for drills), and for dedicated customer vanity domains that opt into Redwood-managed DNS from 300s -> 60s (no change for customer-managed DNS). TTL changes are feature-flagged by Terraform variables so we can stage rollout per region.

2) Health check plumbing: Add/standardize health checks that match the failover controller’s reachability signal (HTTP /healthz/edge and TCP fallback where HTTP is blocked). Health checks include conservative request interval/thresholds to reduce false positives, and are intentionally decoupled from application-level p95 latency signals (those come from metrics; DNS health should only answer “is the edge reachable”).

3) Failover routing policy wiring: Introduce a module that creates/updates the primary/secondary routing policy for regional records (us-east-1 primary, eu-west-1 secondary for the first phase), with explicit “hold-down” notes in tags/annotations for operators. This does not implement automated flips by itself; it ensures the underlying DNS objects exist and are consistent.

4) Outputs for automation + observability: Export record IDs, health check IDs, and key tags as Terraform outputs so the failover controller / incident-bot can link infra objects to audits and dashboards. Added standard tags: reliability_program=regional_failover_automation, service=api-edge, env, region, and owner.

Non-goals: - No provider migration or multi-cloud work.
- No change to customer contract language or SLA.
- No change to app-side routing logic; this is infra-only.

Risk assessment: Lower TTLs can increase query volume and expose resolver caching variance; health checks can cause unintended failover if they’re too sensitive. To mitigate, TTL reductions are staged per region with explicit rollback vars, health checks are conservative, and we keep a minimum TTL floor (20s prod, 10s staging). This PR also avoids introducing any “latency-based” DNS routing to prevent oscillation; failover remains an explicit policy decision via controller logic.

Rollout plan: - Stage 1 (staging): Apply changes in staging and run a rehearsal with the game day checklist. Validate that record propagation and resolver behavior match expectations.
- Stage 2 (prod, limited blast radius): Enable TTL 20s for api.redwoodinference.com in us-east-1/eu-west-1 only. Monitor DNS query rate, NXDOMAIN/servfail rates, edge reachability probes, and error budgets.
- Stage 3 (expand): Roll out to additional regions after the first controlled failover game day confirms success criteria.

Validation: - terraform plan reviewed for prod/us-east-1 and prod/eu-west-1 (no unintended resource recreation).
- Confirmed Route53 health checks are stable in staging under induced partial impairment.
- Confirmed record set changes are in-place updates (no delete/recreate for main A/AAAA alias records).

References: - Linear: ENG-2411 (program), ENG-2418 (signals/alerts), ENG-2421 (game day #1)
- Slack: DNS TTL change risk assessment thread (eng-infra)
- Game day objective: shrink DNS convergence time so controller-driven failover isn’t gated by 60s+ TTLs.

Notes for on-call: If we observe unexpected traffic shifts, rollback is a Terraform var flip restoring TTLs to previous values and disabling the new failover module for affected records. See runbook references in ENG-2421 checklist for monitoring queries and edge reachability during change windows.

title: Refresh Terraform modules CI/CD pipeline and integration tests
preview: Context: Our Confluence CI/CD page for redwood-terraform describes the original alpha pipeline. Since then we’ve added more modules/examples and the integration suite has been flakier and harder to reason about (provider init duplication, inconsistent path filtering, unclear when to run AWS ephemeral tests). This PR refreshes the GitHub Actions workflows and updates the repo’s CI guidance to reflect the current reality. Summary of changes: (1) PR validation now uses a single module/example discovery step that drives a matrix for fmt/validate/tflint and avoids re-running init unnecessarily. (2) Security scanning: keep Checkov as the baseline gate but also upload Trivy IaC (config) results to SARIF for additional signal; Checkov remains the merge-blocking policy for now. (3) Integration tests: previously nightly + manual; now we also auto-run integration tests on PRs when risk classifier flags high-risk changes (NAT/routing, endpoints/DNS, IAM boundaries, SG egress posture). Low/medium risk PRs remain PR-fast by default. (4) AWS auth for integration tests migrated to aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 with tighter session policies and explicit region allowlist; still OIDC, no long-lived keys. title: Expose Dedicated scaling policy (min/max bounds + predictive mode) in modules and examples
preview: This PR updates the redwood-terraform modules and example configs to support the new Dedicated autoscaling policy fields (customer-configurable min/max bounds + predictive mode toggle). The provider/resource schema changes landed in the redwood monorepo; this PR focuses on surfacing the knobs in our Terraform modules, keeping backward compatibility (no behavior change unless fields are set), and documenting expected semantics + feature-flag requirements for predictive mode. Key updates: (1) Dedicated module: add optional inputs for scaling policy (min_replicas, max_replicas, scale_up_cooldown_seconds, scale_down_cooldown_seconds, predictive_mode, predictive_lead_time_seconds). (2) Examples: add a dedicated_deployment example showing cost ceiling + baseline capacity, and an example enabling predictive mode for known daily ramps. (3) Docs: README updates describing precedence (bounds clamp desired replicas), validation (min <= max), and operational notes (predictive mode requires account flag; reactive scaling still applies when forecasts are stale). (4) Outputs: expose effective scaling policy fields in outputs for visibility during plan/apply and for CI drift checks. Notes: predictive_mode is intentionally an opt-in boolean and defaults to false/null to preserve existing behavior; modules use null defaults and conditional blocks to avoid forcing zero-values into the API.

title: Add release tagging and changelog generation
preview: Summary: This PR introduces an opinionated release workflow for redwood-terraform so we can cut alpha module versions consistently (tags + changelog + lightweight automation) without relying on ad-hoc manual steps. This is intended to support the VPC Terraform modules (alpha) program and aligns with the module versioning/release process doc. Motivation: We’ve started landing a steady stream of alpha changes (VPC, endpoints, flow logs, IAM scaffold, CI). Field teams and design partners need pinned versions and readable deltas. Today, we’re manually tagging and writing release notes, and the process is already diverging between maintainers. Goals: (1) One button / one command path to cut a release; (2) deterministic tags (vX.Y.Z); (3) generated CHANGELOG entries based on merged PR metadata; (4) minimal GitHub token scope; (5) guardrails to avoid releasing from dirty or non-main states. Non-goals: publishing to Terraform Registry (external) in alpha; changing module source layout; enforcing conventional commits across all contributors (we’ll encourage, not block). What’s included: - GitHub Actions release workflow: .github/workflows/release.yml supports workflow_dispatch with inputs (version, prerelease flag). It validates we’re on main at a clean commit, creates an annotated tag vX.Y.Z, generates/update CHANGELOG.md, and creates a GitHub Release with the same notes. - Changelog tooling: adds .cliff.toml (git-cliff config) and a scripts/release/generate_changelog.sh wrapper. We generate a scoped section for the version being released and keep CHANGELOG.md append-only. - Version bump automation: scripts/release/bump_version.sh updates module version references where we currently duplicate them (README snippet blocks + examples/* pinned module refs). This keeps examples aligned with the latest release tag. - PR labeling conventions: introduces a lightweight convention to improve changelog quality without blocking merges: labels release-note:feature|fix|security|ops|breaking (breaking allowed but requires explicit acknowledgement). The release script will still work without labels, but output will be less structured. - Safety checks: workflow refuses to run if the repo has uncommitted changes (for local scripts), if version doesn’t match semver, or if the tag already exists. Also enforces the v prefix. - Docs: adds RELEASE.md with step-by-step instructions and a "release dry run" command path for maintainers. Changed areas (high level): - .github/workflows/release.yml (new) - .github/workflows/changelog-check.yml (new; verifies CHANGELOG.md format + that it’s updated when release-note labels are present) - scripts/release/bump_version.sh (new) - scripts/release/generate_changelog.sh (new) - .cliff.toml (new) - CHANGELOG.md (seed file w/ initial alpha notes) - RELEASE.md (new) - Makefile (adds release-dry-run and changelog targets) Notes on permissions/security: The release workflow uses GitHub Actions built-in token with explicit permissions (contents: write). No PATs. The workflow does not assume access to customer accounts or infra. It only tags/releases this repo. It runs only via workflow_dispatch and is restricted to maintainers via repo settings (documented in RELEASE.md). Test plan: - Local: make changelog (verifies git-cliff runs and produces expected output); make release-dry-run (runs semver validation + changelog generation without pushing tags). - CI: ran on this PR: lint + fmt + validate + checkov + tflint all green; workflow yamllint pass. Release workflow itself validated via act-style dry run (documented) and by running workflow on a test tag in a fork. Rollout: once merged, maintainers can cut v0.1.0-alpha.1 (or v0.1.0) following RELEASE.md. We’ll use this for the next design partner drop and update the alpha release notes doc accordingly. Follow-ups: - After we land a few releases, revisit whether we want to enforce release-note labels as a required check. - Consider adding provenance / attestations (SLSA) once we start distributing beyond design partners.

title: Add optional VPN/DX placeholders and routing hooks (no provider assumptions)
preview:
Title: Add optional VPN/DX placeholders and routing hooks (no provider assumptions)

Context
Design partners keep asking, "Where do we plug in our VPN / Direct Connect / Transit Gateway routes?" Our alpha modules intentionally avoid building VPN/DX/TGW resources (provider + enterprise topology specific), but we also need to stop forcing customers to fork the VPC module just to add a couple of routes.

This PR adds:
- Stable outputs for route table IDs and subnet groupings so customers can attach routes externally (VPN/TGW/DX) without modifying our modules.
- Optional, generic "routing hooks" inputs for adding additional routes to the private/public route tables when the target is an ID we already know (e.g., a pre-existing TGW attachment, VGW, or NAT instance) while keeping the module free of assumptions about what creates that target.
- Docs + example showing recommended patterns (customer-owned networking module creates VPN/TGW, then consumes our outputs to add routes).

Scope / non-goals
- NOT creating aws_vpn_gateway / aws_customer_gateway / aws_dx_gateway / aws_ec2_transit_gateway or attachments.
- NOT standardizing on any single enterprise connectivity topology.
- NOT attempting to manage propagation/associations for TGW route tables (customers vary widely).

Implementation details
1) New outputs in modules/vpc
- outputs for:
  - vpc_id
  - private_route_table_ids_by_az
  - public_route_table_ids_by_az
  - private_subnet_ids_by_az
  - public_subnet_ids_by_az
  - intra_subnet_ids_by_az (if enabled)
  - azs

These are intentionally shaped for common enterprise patterns (per-AZ route tables) and are stable identifiers for external modules.

2) Optional route injection (generic)
- Added variable extra_routes with schema:
  - destination_cidr (string)
  - route_table_scope ("private"|"public"|"intra")
  - target_type ("tgw"|"vgw"|"eni"|"nat_instance"|"gateway")
  - target_id (string)

We keep it as a simple switch to set the right aws_route attribute (transit_gateway_id, gateway_id, network_interface_id, etc.) without creating the target resource.

Important: We validate that only one supported target type is set per route entry and we hard-fail on "unknown" to avoid silent misroutes.

3) Example + docs
- Added examples/private-vpc-with-customer-connectivity/
  - shows a placeholder customer module creating a TGW attachment (commented/pseudocode) and then adding routes via our outputs.
  - callouts for: DNS expectations, ECR endpoints, and making sure installer can reach required services.

Why this shape?
- SE feedback (Markus/Colin): customers want a clear "attachment point" without us dictating whether it’s VPN, DX, TGW, or something else.
- Security feedback (Marcus): we should not implicitly open egress or create VPN constructs; keep connectivity owned by customer networking team.
- Ops feedback (Nina): avoid forcing route table recreation (high risk) and avoid any behavior that changes NAT strategy.

Files changed (high level)
- modules/vpc/variables.tf (extra_routes input + validation)
- modules/vpc/outputs.tf (route table + subnet outputs)
- modules/vpc/routes.tf (optional aws_route resources per scope)
- examples/private-vpc-with-customer-connectivity/main.tf
- examples/private-vpc-with-customer-connectivity/README.md
- docs/extension-points.md (new section: Customer connectivity / VPN / DX hooks)

Testing
- terraform fmt / validate
- tflint
- checkov (no new high severity findings)
- integration test harness: ephemeral VPC apply/destroy (routing hooks exercised with dummy ENI target in test)

Notes for reviewers
- I intentionally did not add any new required variables. Default behavior is unchanged.
- Outputs are additive; no renames.
- Route injection only creates routes when extra_routes is non-empty.

Review conversation (abridged)

Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): Can we make the docs explicit that this is where DX/VPN plugs in? Our pilots keep asking for "Direct Connect support" and we need to explain we support it via hooks, not by creating it.
Bruno Silva (author): Yep. Added a short section in docs/extension-points.md and an example directory. Also added wording: "customer-owned connectivity resources".

Victor Alvarez (Networking): output names should be consistent with endpoints module naming. Prefer *_ids_by_az and also provide azs output so consumers don’t have to recompute.
Bruno Silva: Updated outputs to *_ids_by_az and added azs.

Marcus Lin (Security): extra_routes target_type list is OK, but make sure we validate we don’t accidentally set multiple route target fields. Also, call out that we don’t manage route propagation.
Bruno Silva: Added input validation and README note. No propagation logic.

Nina Petrova (Private Deployments): Please ensure we don’t force route table replacement for existing users when they adopt this module version.
Bruno Silva: Confirmed in plan output: additive only. No diff unless extra_routes set.

Irene Choi (Infra): CI runtime increased by ~2 min from the new integration test case. Fine for now but keep it minimal.
Bruno Silva: Acknowledged; kept it to a single route injection case.

CI
- redwood-terraform / validate: pass
- tflint: pass
- checkov: pass
- integration-tests (ephemeral-vpc): pass

Merge
- Squashed and merged to main.
- Follow-up: I’ll open a small PR to add a snippet to the main VPC module README linking to docs/extension-points.md (missed in this PR).

title: CI: add tflint + checkov gates; pin Terraform/AWS provider versions for reproducible builds
preview:
Summary
This PR hardens the `redwood-terraform` alpha module repo by adding Terraform linting + security scanning in CI and by pinning Terraform/AWS provider versions to improve reproducibility across dev machines and CI runners.

Motivation
We’ve had a few sources of drift already during dogfood: different AWS provider patch versions producing slightly different plans, and “looks fine locally” modules that fail basic best-practice checks once they hit customer environments. Since these modules are explicitly meant to encode Redwood-validated defaults, we should catch issues early and keep outputs stable.

What’s included
- Provider + Terraform version pinning:
  - Add explicit `required_version` and `required_providers` constraints across modules/examples.
  - Standardize on AWS provider `~> 5.0` with a minimum patch level validated in sandbox.
  - Add/refresh `.terraform.lock.hcl` for the example environment(s) to stabilize CI.
  - Document the compatibility matrix in the repo README (Terraform + AWS provider versions).
- TFLint:
  - Add `.tflint.hcl` with the AWS ruleset enabled.
  - Add `make lint` target + `tflint --recursive` invocation for modules + examples.
  - Configure a small allowlist of rules we’re intentionally not enforcing yet (alpha) with TODOs and owners.
- Checkov:
  - Add `make scan` target using Checkov for Terraform (IaC security baseline).
  - Configure CI to fail on HIGH/CRITICAL by default.
  - Add a minimal set of targeted suppressions for known false positives with explicit justifications (e.g., certain “encryption” checks not applicable for resources we don’t create in alpha).
- CI wiring:
  - Update GitHub Actions workflow to run: fmt -> validate -> tflint -> checkov.
  - Cache plugin downloads where possible to keep PR runtime sane.

How to run locally
- `make fmt && make validate`
- `make lint` (requires tflint; see README for install)
- `make scan` (requires checkov)

Notes on version pinning
- We’re pinning versions to reduce “plan churn” and to ensure the alpha behaves consistently for design partners.
- We’re not committing lockfiles per-module yet; only the example environments include `.terraform.lock.hcl` since they’re what CI executes end-to-end. Module consumers can choose whether to commit lockfiles.

CI issues encountered (and fixes)
- Run 1 failed: `tflint --recursive` flagged missing `required_providers` blocks in 3 modules; fixed by adding `versions.tf` files consistently.
- Run 2 failed: Checkov flagged public egress warnings on NAT Gateway route tables in the example. This is expected for the default (private subnets via NAT). Added suppressions with rationale and linked to the alpha security posture doc.
- Run 3 failed: Provider constraint mismatch between root example and module constraints (`>= 5.12.0` vs `~> 5.0`). Normalized to `>= 5.12.0, < 6.0.0` across the repo.
- Run 4 passed after updating the lockfile and ensuring CI uses the same Terraform version as documented.

Security review highlights
- Marcus requested that every Checkov suppression include:
  - A short, specific justification
  - A reference to a tracking item for removal (Linear)
  - Avoid blanket suppressions at directory level
  Addressed by scoping suppressions to resource blocks and adding TODOs.

Release impact
No functional infra behavior changes intended, but stricter CI gates may block PRs that previously passed. This is expected.

Follow-ups
- Evaluate adding `tfsec` or moving to a single consolidated IaC scanner; defer until we see signal/noise from Checkov in alpha.
- Consider enforcing provider pinning in examples only (current) vs all modules (kept constraints in modules, lockfile only in examples).

Changelog
- Added: CI lint + security scan stages (tflint, checkov)
- Added: provider + Terraform version constraints and documentation
- Fixed: inconsistent provider constraints across modules/examples

Screenshots/log excerpts
- Checkov gate example (after suppressions): HIGH/CRITICAL = 0, MEDIUM = 6 (non-blocking)
- tflint: 0 issues across modules + examples

  - Add explicit `required_version` and `required_providers` constraints across modules/examples.
  - Standardize on AWS provider `~> 5.0` with a minimum patch level validated in sandbox.
  - Add/refresh `.terraform.lock.hcl` for the example environment(s) to stabilize CI.
  - Document the compatibility matrix in the repo README (Terraform + AWS provider versions).
- TFLint:
  - Add `.tflint.hcl` with the AWS ruleset enabled.
  - Add `make lint` target + `tflint --recursive` invocation for modules + examples.
  - Configure a small allowlist of rules we’re intentionally not enforcing yet (alpha) with TODOs and owners.
- Checkov:
  - Add `make scan` target using Checkov for Terraform (IaC security baseline).
  - Configure CI to fail on HIGH/CRITICAL by default.
  - Add a minimal set of targeted suppressions for known false positives with explicit justifications (e.g., certain “encryption” checks not applicable for resources we don’t create in alpha).
- CI wiring:
  - Update GitHub Actions workflow to run: fmt -> validate -> tflint -> checkov.
  - Cache plugin downloads where possible to keep PR runtime sane.

How to run locally
- `make fmt && make validate`
- `make lint` (requires tflint; see README for install)
- `make scan` (requires checkov)

Notes on version pinning
- We’re pinning versions to reduce “plan churn” and to ensure the alpha behaves consistently for design partners.
- We’re not committing lockfiles per-module yet; only the example environments include `.terraform.lock.hcl` since they’re what CI executes end-to-end. Module consumers can choose whether to commit lockfiles.

CI issues encountered (and fixes)
- Run 1 failed: `tflint --recursive` flagged missing `required_providers` blocks in 3 modules; fixed by adding `versions.tf` files consistently.
- Run 2 failed: Checkov flagged public egress warnings on NAT Gateway route tables in the example. This is expected for the default (private subnets via NAT). Added suppressions with rationale and linked to the alpha security posture doc.
- Run 3 failed: Provider constraint mismatch between root example and module constraints (`>= 5.12.0` vs `~> 5.0`). Normalized to `>= 5.12.0, < 6.0.0` across the repo.
- Run 4 passed after updating the lockfile and ensuring CI uses the same Terraform version as documented.

Security review highlights
- Marcus requested that every Checkov suppression include:
  - A short, specific justification
  - A reference to a tracking item for removal (Linear)
  - Avoid blanket suppressions at directory level
  Addressed by scoping suppressions to resource blocks and adding TODOs.

Release impact
No functional infra behavior changes intended, but stricter CI gates may block PRs that previously passed. This is expected.

Follow-ups
- Evaluate adding `tfsec` or moving to a single consolidated IaC scanner; defer until we see signal/noise from Checkov in alpha.
- Consider enforcing provider pinning in examples only (current) vs all modules (kept constraints in modules, lockfile only in examples).

Changelog
- Added: CI lint + security scan stages (tflint, checkov)
- Added: provider + Terraform version constraints and documentation
- Fixed: inconsistent provider constraints across modules/examples

Screenshots/log excerpts
- Checkov gate example (after suppressions): HIGH/CRITICAL = 0, MEDIUM = 6 (non-blocking)
- tflint: 0 issues across modules + examples

title: Add integration test harness for ephemeral VPC (create/validate/destroy)
preview: Summary: This PR introduces an integration test harness that provisions an ephemeral AWS VPC using our alpha modules, validates a small set of expected invariants/outputs, and then tears everything down. The goal is to catch provider/module drift and unsafe defaults earlier than design partner pilots (e.g., subnet mapping, NAT assumptions, endpoint/DNS regressions). Motivation/Context: Unit-level terraform validate/tflint/checkov are necessary but not sufficient; we’ve already had issues that only show up once AWS resources exist (e.g., AZ assumptions, route association wiring, endpoint private DNS behavior). This harness is a minimal “smoke” environment that deploys modules the way customers will via examples, with assertions for the outputs the installer expects downstream. What’s included: (1) A Go-based Terratest suite under test/integration/ that runs terraform init/apply against examples/integration/ephemeral-vpc and performs basic assertions. (2) A dedicated example config (ephemeral-vpc) that composes modules/vpc + modules/endpoints + modules/logging with the smallest viable set of variables. (3) GitHub Actions workflow .github/workflows/integration-tests-ephemeral-vpc.yml that assumes an AWS role in the sandbox account via OIDC, runs the tests, and always attempts teardown (defer destroy). (4) Guardrails: unique name prefix per run, TTL tags on all resources, and a best-effort cleanup job to reduce orphan risk on cancelled runs. (5) Documentation in the repo README for how/when to run integration tests and the expected CI cost/time. CI/Cost/Runtime notes: Running this on every PR is expensive (AWS costs + ~18–30 min runtime depending on region/AZ capacity). Current proposal in this PR: run nightly on main + allow manual workflow_dispatch for maintainers and release candidates. We still keep fmt/validate/tflint/checkov gating per-PR (already in progress under ENG-4837 / PR-18503). Validation assertions (current set): VPC is created with expected CIDR; public/private subnets count matches inputs; private subnets have a route to NAT (when enabled); VPC endpoints created for S3/ECR API/ECR DKR/STS/CloudWatch Logs; endpoint security group is present; flow logs destination is created when enabled; key outputs are non-empty (vpc_id, private_subnet_ids, endpoint_sg_id). Notably we’re not asserting exact route table IDs or full endpoint policy JSON to avoid brittle tests; we’re targeting invariants that map to common pilot failures. Follow-ups (not in this PR): Add a second scenario for “no-NAT + endpoints-only” topology; add an assertion for private DNS settings (enabled/disabled) once we lock defaults with Security; wire a weekly budget report for sandbox account spend; consider moving from Terratest to terraform test once we’re on a baseline Terraform version across customers. Screenshots/Logs: See workflow run output for a full terraform plan/apply log; by default we redact AWS account IDs and do not print full state. Reviewer checklist: Please focus on (a) safety of the teardown paths, (b) whether assertions align with what the installer actually needs, and (c) whether the workflow scheduling is acceptable for alpha (nightly + manual).

title: Add VPC Flow Logs defaults + configurable log destinations (CloudWatch/S3)
preview:
Title: Add VPC Flow Logs defaults + configurable log destinations (CloudWatch/S3)

Context
We’ve had repeated pilot friction around “are we capturing network telemetry by default, and where does it go?” For Redwood Private VPC deployments we want sane, audit-friendly defaults while still letting customers route logs to their own sinks (CloudWatch or S3 today; SIEM forwarding remains out-of-scope for these modules).

This PR introduces a new flow logs submodule and wires it into the example envs:
- Default-on VPC Flow Logs for the created VPC
- Destination options: CloudWatch Logs or S3 (customer-owned bucket supported)
- Explicit retention/lifecycle inputs with conservative defaults and clear cost/audit trade-offs

What changed
1) New module: modules/flow-logs
- Creates aws_flow_log for the VPC
- Supports traffic_type = ALL by default (input override allowed)
- Destination selection:
  - cloudwatch: creates/uses a CloudWatch Log Group + IAM role for vpc-flow-logs.amazonaws.com
  - s3: writes to S3 bucket ARN (either module-managed or customer-provided)
- Default format uses the AWS recommended extended fields (includes: account-id, vpc-id, subnet-id, instance-id, interface-id, srcaddr/dstaddr, srcport/dstport, protocol, tcp-flags, packets/bytes, start/end, action, log-status)

2) Retention/lifecycle defaults + inputs
- CloudWatch retention default set to 90 days (var.flow_logs_cloudwatch_retention_days) to meet common audit expectations; customers can increase/decrease.
- S3 lifecycle default set to 365 days (var.flow_logs_s3_expiration_days) when using module-managed bucket; if customer supplies their own bucket, lifecycle is not mutated.
- Both destinations support optional KMS encryption:
  - CloudWatch: kms_key_id on the log group
  - S3: bucket SSE-KMS when module manages the bucket; customer buckets are assumed to be already compliant

3) Example updates
- examples/alpha/private-vpc now enables flow logs by default and demonstrates both destination types via commented tfvars blocks.

4) Docs
- README updates for flow logs behavior, destination trade-offs, and “customer-owned bucket” expectations (encryption, access logs, lifecycle handled by customer).

Key implementation notes
- We deliberately do not create CloudWatch subscription filters / SIEM forwarding here. The module outputs log_group_name/log_group_arn (CloudWatch) or bucket_arn/prefix (S3) so customers can attach their own forwarding pipelines.
- For S3 destination we include an optional log_prefix to keep multi-env buckets tidy.
- For CloudWatch destination we create a least-privilege IAM role allowing only logs:CreateLogStream and logs:PutLogEvents to the specific log group.

Illustrative snippet (module interface)
- enable_flow_logs (bool, default true)
- flow_logs_destination (string: "cloudwatch"|"s3", default "cloudwatch")
- flow_logs_cloudwatch_retention_days (number, default 90)
- flow_logs_s3_bucket_arn (string, optional; if set, module will not create bucket)
- flow_logs_s3_expiration_days (number, default 365; only applies to module-managed bucket)
- flow_logs_kms_key_id (string, optional)

Review discussion highlights
- Kevin Osei (Security): requested we avoid silently writing logs into a Redwood-managed bucket by default; agreed. Default destination remains CloudWatch (customer account) and any S3 bucket is explicitly customer-owned unless they opt into module-managed bucket creation.
- Allison Grant (Observability): asked that we use traffic_type=ALL by default and include log-status/action fields to ease troubleshooting. Incorporated.
- Marcus Lin (Security Eng): requested KMS hooks for both CloudWatch and S3 paths and a clear doc note that customer-owned buckets must enforce encryption + least-privilege bucket policy. Added.
- Irene Choi (Infra): raised cost concerns about CloudWatch retention defaults. We kept default at 90 days to align with typical audit requirements for regulated pilots; customers can set lower values, and docs call out expected cost drivers.

CI
- terraform fmt/validate: pass
- tflint: pass
- checkov: pass (initial failure on S3 bucket public access block in the module-managed bucket path; fixed by adding PublicAccessBlock + explicit bucket ownership controls)
- integration tests: skipped (not yet wired for this module in ENG-4860 harness; follow-up ticket to extend ephemeral VPC test to assert flow log resource creation)

Files/areas changed (high level)
- modules/flow-logs/* (new)
- modules/vpc/outputs.tf (expose vpc_id for flow logs wiring in examples)
- examples/alpha/private-vpc/* (enable + tfvars documentation)
- docs/README.md (new section: Flow Logs)

Merge
- Merged via squash to main.

Follow-ups
- ENG-4860: extend ephemeral integration test to assert flow logs creation and destination-specific outputs
- Add optional CloudWatch log group name override (if customers want to centralize logs) after first design partner feedback

title: Add IAM scaffold for Redwood Private installer execution role (with permission boundaries)
preview:
## Summary
This PR introduces an alpha IAM scaffolding module to support running `redwood-private-installer` in customer AWS accounts using a dedicated execution role. The module creates an IAM role + inline policy (or wires to an existing role), and is designed to be compatible with customer permission boundary requirements from day one.

The goal is not to encode every enterprise IAM topology, but to provide a Redwood-validated default that works for most pilots and gives security teams a clear review surface.

## Why
During discovery/pilots we repeatedly hit delays around: (1) "what IAM does the installer need?" and (2) "how do we enforce least privilege and boundaries?". Providing a standardized scaffold reduces iteration time and makes the rest of the VPC module suite easier to adopt.

## What changed
- New module: `modules/iam_installer_execution_role`
  - Creates `aws_iam_role.installer_execution` with configurable trust policy
  - Attaches an inline policy document generated via `aws_iam_policy_document` (split into statements for readability and checkov hygiene)
  - Optional permission boundary support (`permission_boundary_arn` input)
  - Outputs: `installer_execution_role_arn`, `installer_execution_role_name`, `installer_execution_role_unique_id`, `installer_execution_policy_json`
- New example wiring in `examples/private_vpc_alpha` to demonstrate expected usage and naming.
- Documentation:
  - Adds README section describing recommended boundary model and how customers can supply an existing pre-approved role.

## Inputs (high level)
- `create_role` (bool): default true
- `existing_role_arn` (string): if provided and `create_role=false`, module only validates/outputs
- `permission_boundary_arn` (string): optional but strongly recommended for enterprise pilots
- `trusted_principals` (list): AWS principals allowed to assume role (defaults to the account root; can be narrowed)
- `tags` (map): required by our internal standards and makes audit review easier

## Policy scope (alpha)
The inline policy is intentionally conservative and focused on installer needs for EKS + supporting AWS primitives:
- EC2/VPC describe and limited mutate operations required for cluster/security group wiring
- EKS create/update/describe as needed by installer
- CloudWatch Logs (create log group/stream + put events) for installer-run logs (optional, can be disabled)
- IAM `PassRole` restricted to roles created by the installer (conditioned by path + boundary requirement) to reduce privilege escalation risk

> Note: We expect to tighten/adjust based on installer preflight checks and real pilot behavior. The policy is generated from structured documents so future diffs are reviewable.

## Security posture / requested changes addressed
Requested by Security Eng (Marcus/Hanae) and incorporated here:
- Permission boundary support is first-class (role is created with boundary if provided).
- Trust policy is configurable; docs recommend narrowing to a specific CI principal / human breakglass role.
- Reduced wildcard usage and added conditions where feasible to satisfy checkov and internal review standards.
- Set `max_session_duration` to 1h by default.
- Added explicit tagging on IAM resources.

## Validation
- `terraform fmt` / `terraform validate`
- `tflint` (aws ruleset)
- `checkov`
- Manual plan against sandbox account (no apply) to validate policy renders and role attaches correctly.

## Follow-ups
- Installer team: align preflight checks with these permissions and document any required deltas (tracked under ENG-4869).
- Docs: add a "recommended boundary policy snippet" customers can reuse (separate PR).

## Screenshots / snippets
Example output policy excerpt (trimmed):
- Allow `eks:*` on cluster resources under the configured name prefix
- Allow `iam:PassRole` only when the target role has the configured permission boundary

No breaking changes; module is additive and optional.

title: Add VPC module with subnet + routing defaults
preview: Summary: This PR implements the core `modules/vpc` module for the Private VPC Terraform modules (alpha). The goal is to provide a Redwood-validated default VPC layout suitable for Redwood Private deployments that run on EKS (assumed) with private worker nodes and conservative routing defaults. This module focuses on: VPC creation, public/private subnet layout across AZs, route tables, and a NAT strategy with explicit trade-offs documented. Motivation: Technical discovery/pilots keep getting blocked on basic networking variability (subnet layouts, route tables, NAT/egress assumptions). We want a default that works for most enterprise VPC baselines while remaining composable with future modules (endpoints/logging/iam) and customer-specific connectivity (VPN/DX) without forcing premature complexity. What’s included: (1) VPC with DNS enabled by default: `enable_dns_support=true` and `enable_dns_hostnames=true`. (2) Subnet layout: public + private subnets per AZ (min 2 AZs recommended). CIDR splitting is opinionated but overridable via explicit subnet CIDRs. (3) Routing: public subnets route `0.0.0.0/0` to an Internet Gateway; private subnets route `0.0.0.0/0` to NAT Gateway(s). (4) NAT strategy defaults: default is NAT Gateway per AZ (`one_nat_gateway_per_az=true`) to avoid cross-AZ dependency and improve fault isolation; optional `single_nat_gateway=true` for cost-sensitive sandboxes/dev. (5) Tags for EKS compatibility: default EKS subnet role tags and cluster discovery tags are supported. By default we tag public subnets with `kubernetes.io/role/elb=1` and private subnets with `kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb=1`. We also support `kubernetes.io/cluster/<cluster_name>=shared` when `eks_cluster_name` is provided (alpha assumes cluster created elsewhere; this module only prepares network). (6) Example environment: `examples/private-eks-vpc` shows minimal inputs, expected outputs, and common overrides for enterprise CIDR requirements. Public interface (high level): Required inputs: `name`, `vpc_cidr`, `azs` (list). Common inputs: `public_subnet_cidrs`, `private_subnet_cidrs` (optional, otherwise calculated), `single_nat_gateway` (bool), `one_nat_gateway_per_az` (bool), `eks_cluster_name` (optional), `tags` (map). Outputs: `vpc_id`, `public_subnet_ids`, `private_subnet_ids`, `public_route_table_ids`, `private_route_table_ids`, `nat_gateway_ids`, `igw_id`, `azs`. Design notes / constraints (important for Redwood Private + EKS): - This module does NOT create VPC Endpoints yet. For truly “no public egress” environments, you will need endpoints from the follow-up endpoints module (tracked separately). For now, NAT provides outbound access for pulling images, talking to AWS APIs, etc. - EKS private endpoint: EKS API endpoint access mode is not set here; however, for Private deployments we assume customers will configure private endpoint access and ensure VPC DNS/resolver behavior is stable. We explicitly enable VPC DNS support/hostnames to reduce the most common private endpoint failure mode. - Ingress/egress policy: This module deliberately does not create security groups. SG patterns are split into a separate module/PR so we can iterate on least-privilege without blocking VPC composition. - AZ assumptions: the module behaves best with >=2 AZs. If you pass 1 AZ, NAT-per-AZ and CIDR calculations still work, but we consider it out of scope for “production-like” alpha deployments (documented in README). - IPv6: not included in alpha. - Isolated/data subnets: not included yet; can be added later as an extension (DB/cache patterns) without breaking the current contract. title: Add VPC endpoints module + private DNS options for Redwood Private (alpha)
preview:
## Summary
This adds an opinionated `vpc-endpoints` Terraform module for the Redwood Private VPC footprint (alpha). The module creates a baseline set of Gateway + Interface endpoints commonly required in fully-private subnets (no public egress) and exposes explicit inputs for private DNS behavior.

Primary motivation: reduce pilot failures related to image pulls / registry access / AWS API access in private subnets by making endpoints part of our reference modules, while staying compatible with customer DNS strategies (Route 53 Resolver, custom forwarders, split-horizon, etc.).

## What’s included
### New module: `modules/vpc-endpoints`
Creates endpoints in the provided VPC + subnets:
- **Gateway endpoint**: S3 (`com.amazonaws.<region>.s3`) with route table association(s)
- **Interface endpoints** (baseline):
  - ECR API (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api`)
  - ECR DKR (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr`)
  - STS (`com.amazonaws.<region>.sts`)
  - CloudWatch Logs (`com.amazonaws.<region>.logs`)
  - CloudWatch Monitoring (`com.amazonaws.<region>.monitoring`)
  - EC2 (`com.amazonaws.<region>.ec2`) (needed for some node lifecycle flows depending on cluster config)

Also provides a shared security group for endpoint ENIs (default: allow 443 from private subnet CIDRs + any additional CIDRs passed via inputs).

### Private DNS controls
We’ve had multiple pilot/customer environments where enabling Private DNS on interface endpoints causes unexpected resolution changes due to existing Route 53 Resolver rules / custom DNS forwarders.

Inputs:
- `interface_private_dns_enabled` (bool, default **true**): global default for interface endpoints
- `interface_private_dns_overrides` (map(string)=bool): per-service override keyed by short name (e.g. `ecr_api`, `ecr_dkr`, `sts`, `logs`, `monitoring`, `ec2`)
- `enable_*` toggles per endpoint (e.g. `enable_ecr`, `enable_sts`, `enable_logs`) to support environments that have a subset already provisioned by central networking teams

Behavior:
- If an endpoint is enabled and its private DNS is enabled, we set `private_dns_enabled = true` for that endpoint.
- If disabled, we still provision the endpoint but do not flip private DNS; customers can manage DNS explicitly (and the installer can use endpoint-specific DNS names if needed).

### Outputs
- `gateway_endpoint_id_s3`
- `interface_endpoint_ids` (map)
- `interface_endpoint_dns_entries` (map of lists; useful for troubleshooting)
- `endpoints_security_group_id`

## Changes in examples
- `examples/alpha/private-vpc/` now wires in the endpoints module and passes the private subnets + route tables.
- Added a short README section explaining the trade-offs of private DNS and how to debug resolution (dig against VPC resolver, check resolver rules, confirm endpoint private hosted zone association).

## CI updates
- GitHub Actions now runs `terraform fmt -check` and `terraform validate` for:
  - `modules/vpc-endpoints`
  - `examples/alpha/private-vpc` (init with `-backend=false`)
- This is intentionally lightweight; integration tests come later in ENG-4860.

## Notes / constraints
- This module assumes AWS provider >= 5.x and Terraform >= 1.5 (consistent with repo baseline).
- Subnets passed to interface endpoints should be private subnets with appropriate NACLs allowing 443 to the endpoint ENIs.
- We avoid hardcoding endpoint policies by default. Policy can be passed via `interface_endpoint_policy_json` / `s3_gateway_endpoint_policy_json` if security teams require explicit allowlists.

## Testing
- `terraform validate` across module + example
- Manual: applied in sandbox (single region) and verified:
  - ECR endpoints resolve to private IPs when private DNS enabled
  - `aws ecr get-login-password` succeeds from an instance in a private subnet without NAT
  - CloudWatch Logs PutLogEvents succeeds from private subnet

## Checklist
- [x] Module docs added
- [x] Example updated
- [x] CI validation updated
- [x] Security review requested

## Screenshots / logs
N/A

---

### Reviewer discussion highlights
**Marcus Lin (Security):** “Please avoid implicit broad access via endpoint policies; can we default to a restrictive policy?”

**Bruno Silva (Author):** “AWS default endpoint policy is effectively full access within account context; we can’t safely infer customer allowlists. Added optional policy JSON inputs and documented recommended patterns (limit actions + principals, and restrict to required ARNs).”

**Hanae Suzuki (Security):** “Private DNS defaults to true will surprise some enterprises with existing resolver rules. Please add a global flag and per-service overrides, and call out the risk in README.”

**Bruno Silva (Author):** “Implemented `interface_private_dns_enabled` + `interface_private_dns_overrides` and added ‘DNS gotchas’ section to module README + example.”

**Victor Alvarez (Networking):** “S3 should be gateway endpoint by default. For route table associations: accept list to support shared route tables across private subnets.”

**Bruno Silva (Author):** “Done—`s3_route_table_ids` is a list; example passes private route tables.”

**Nina Petrova (Private Deployments):** “Make sure ECR includes both ecr.api and ecr.dkr; we’ve seen partial setups fail during image pulls.”

**Bruno Silva (Author):** “Both included under `enable_ecr` (creates both endpoints), with per-endpoint private DNS overrides supported.”

**Sahana Iyer (CI):** “CI should validate the example too; module-only validate won’t catch wiring issues.”

**Bruno Silva (Author):** “Added `examples/alpha/private-vpc` init/validate step with backend disabled.”

---

## Merge notes
Squash merged after final security + networking approvals. Follow-ups tracked in ENG-4869 (installer preflight) to ensure we warn clearly when endpoints are missing or private DNS is off and the environment expects it.

title: Add security group patterns for private ingress/egress (EKS + installer-friendly outputs)
preview: This PR introduces an opinionated set of Security Group (SG) modules/patterns for Redwood Private AWS deployments (alpha). The intent is to reduce pilot variability and common discovery churn by shipping Redwood-validated defaults for: (1) EKS worker/node SG behavior (private nodes), (2) load balancer SGs for internal ingress (ALB/NLB) and controlled cross-subnet traffic, and (3) egress policy that works in a mostly-private VPC with VPC endpoints (ECR/S3/STS/CloudWatch Logs, etc.). Key goals: keep the default posture secure (no accidental broad public egress), but avoid breaking real-world installs where customers still need proxy/NAT-based egress or have non-standard endpoint/DNS setups. The modules are designed to be composable and to expose controlled extension points (additional egress CIDRs, additional destination SGs, and an explicit opt-in for “wide egress”). Summary of changes: Added new module `modules/security-groups` with submodules `eks_nodes`, `internal_lb`, and `egress`. Added a shared `rules` helper to centralize common TCP/UDP rules and keep diffs readable. Default node SG: ingress from cluster SG (EKS-managed), ingress from internal LB SG on app ports (configurable list, default 443), and egress constrained to VPC endpoints (via prefix lists and endpoint ENI SGs) + DNS + NTP (optional). Default internal LB SG: ingress from allowlisted CIDRs / SGs (supports customer VPN/DX CIDRs) and egress only to node SG on configured target ports. Default egress SG pattern: supports two modes: (a) “endpoint-only” (default) where 443 egress is restricted to endpoint prefix lists and/or endpoint SG IDs, and (b) “wide” where 0.0.0.0/0 egress on 443 is allowed for customers who require NAT/proxy access during bring-up (explicit input `enable_wide_egress = true`, default false). Outputs: `node_security_group_id`, `internal_lb_security_group_id`, `egress_security_group_id`, plus lists for per-rule debugging to make installer preflight messages actionable. Example usage added to `examples/alpha/private-eks-vpc/` and referenced in README. Notes: This PR does not attempt to model every enterprise firewall topology; instead, it standardizes the baseline and provides extension points. In particular, customers with explicit proxy egress can attach the egress SG to proxy ENIs or override via `additional_egress_*` inputs. Checklist: [x] Terraform fmt/validate; [x] tflint; [x] checkov (with documented suppressions where appropriate); [x] example plan applies in sandbox; [x] docs updated for supported extension points; [x] aligns with installer expectations for private image pulls (ECR/S3/STSv2).

title: Private: add version pinning, upgrade window controls, and artifact source configuration
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds explicit Terraform inputs for Redwood Private lifecycle management: (1) version pinning (N-1 → N upgrade default), (2) upgrade window configuration to encode operator intent and reduce accidental upgrades outside of maintenance windows, and (3) artifact source configuration to make artifact provenance explicit (registry/chart repo URLs) and unblock future offline mirror work.

These changes are intentionally additive and default to today’s behavior when unset.

## Motivation
We’re rolling out a safer, more deterministic Private upgrade workflow (plan + prechecks + backup/rollback). Terraform is a primary entry point for VPC Private installs, and we need a consistent way to:
- Pin the target Redwood Private platform version across environments
- Avoid accidental upgrades by making a maintenance window an explicit input (and validated when provided)
- Standardize where artifacts come from so the installer can validate availability and we can later support mirrors/air-gapped flows

Related:
- Linear: ENG-4821 (Private upgrade plan v1), PM-812 (GA requirements)
- Support: SUP-18488 (customer asking for rollback + clearer upgrade procedure)

## What changed
### 1) Version pinning
New variables on the Private modules:
- `redwood_private_version` (string, optional): pins the platform version deployed by the module
- `redwood_private_channel` (string, optional, default "stable"): allows future channel selection without breaking interfaces

Behavior:
- If `redwood_private_version` is set, we pass it through to the installer/helm values and include it in outputs.
- If unset, the module behaves as before (uses the default recommended version path defined by the module release).

### 2) Upgrade window inputs
Adds optional upgrade window variables:
- `upgrade_window_start` (string, optional, RFC3339 local-time-like "HH:MM" form)
- `upgrade_window_end` (string, optional)
- `upgrade_window_timezone` (string, optional, default "UTC")

Validation:
- If any of the window vars are set, all required parts must be set.
- Rejects obvious invalid formats (e.g., missing leading zeros) using `validation { condition = ... }`.

Note: Terraform cannot perfectly validate timezone names across providers without extra deps; we validate non-empty and document expected IANA tz database values.

### 3) Artifact source configuration (online)
Adds variables to make artifact sources explicit:
- `artifact_registry` (string, optional): container registry hostname / base
- `artifact_chart_repo` (string, optional): helm repo base URL
- `artifact_image_pull_secret_name` (string, optional): existing secret name (no secret material in TF state)

Defaults preserve current behavior (Redwood-hosted endpoints).

## Implementation notes
- All new inputs are plumbed through module outputs so operators can inspect effective settings from Terraform state.
- Added minimal guardrails to avoid storing credentials: we only accept secret *names* and rely on cluster-side secret creation.
- Updated docs and examples to show pinning + window configuration.

### Example usage
```
module "redwood_private" {
  source = "github.com/redwoodinference/redwood-terraform//modules/private-vpc"

  redwood_private_version   = "1.14.3"
  redwood_private_channel   = "stable"

  upgrade_window_start      = "02:00"
  upgrade_window_end        = "04:00"
  upgrade_window_timezone   = "America/Los_Angeles"

  artifact_registry         = "registry.redwood.example.com"
  artifact_chart_repo       = "https://charts.redwood.example.com/private"
  artifact_image_pull_secret_name = "redwood-regcred"
}
```

## Changed areas / files
- `modules/private-vpc/variables.tf`: add new variables + validations

text:
## Summary
This PR adds explicit Terraform inputs for Redwood Private lifecycle management: (1) version pinning (N-1 → N upgrade default), (2) upgrade window configuration to encode operator intent and reduce accidental upgrades outside of maintenance windows, and (3) artifact source configuration to make artifact provenance explicit (registry/chart repo URLs) and unblock future offline mirror work.

These changes are intentionally additive and default to today’s behavior when unset.

## Motivation
We’re rolling out a safer, more deterministic Private upgrade workflow (plan + prechecks + backup/rollback). Terraform is a primary entry point for VPC Private installs, and we need a consistent way to:
- Pin the target Redwood Private platform version across environments
- Avoid accidental upgrades by making a maintenance window an explicit input (and validated when provided)
- Standardize where artifacts come from so the installer can validate availability and we can later support mirrors/air-gapped flows

Related:
- Linear: ENG-4821 (Private upgrade plan v1), PM-812 (GA requirements)
- Support: SUP-18488 (customer asking for rollback + clearer upgrade procedure)

## What changed
### 1) Version pinning
New variables on the Private modules:
- `redwood_private_version` (string, optional): pins the platform version deployed by the module
- `redwood_private_channel` (string, optional, default "stable"): allows future channel selection without breaking interfaces

Behavior:
- If `redwood_private_version` is set, we pass it through to the installer/helm values and include it in outputs.
- If unset, the module behaves as before (uses the default recommended version path defined by the module release).

### 2) Upgrade window inputs
Adds optional upgrade window variables:
- `upgrade_window_start` (string, optional, RFC3339 local-time-like "HH:MM" form)
- `upgrade_window_end` (string, optional)
- `upgrade_window_timezone` (string, optional, default "UTC")

Validation:
- If any of the window vars are set, all required parts must be set.
- Rejects obvious invalid formats (e.g., missing leading zeros) using `validation { condition = ... }`.

Note: Terraform cannot perfectly validate timezone names across providers without extra deps; we validate non-empty and document expected IANA tz database values.

### 3) Artifact source configuration (online)
Adds variables to make artifact sources explicit:
- `artifact_registry` (string, optional): container registry hostname / base
- `artifact_chart_repo` (string, optional): helm repo base URL
- `artifact_image_pull_secret_name` (string, optional): existing secret name (no secret material in TF state)

Defaults preserve current behavior (Redwood-hosted endpoints).

## Implementation notes
- All new inputs are plumbed through module outputs so operators can inspect effective settings from Terraform state.
- Added minimal guardrails to avoid storing credentials: we only accept secret *names* and rely on cluster-side secret creation.
- Updated docs and examples to show pinning + window configuration.

### Example usage
```
module "redwood_private" {
  source = "github.com/redwoodinference/redwood-terraform//modules/private-vpc"

  redwood_private_version   = "1.14.3"
  redwood_private_channel   = "stable"

  upgrade_window_start      = "02:00"
  upgrade_window_end        = "04:00"
  upgrade_window_timezone   = "America/Los_Angeles"

  artifact_registry         = "registry.redwood.example.com"
  artifact_chart_repo       = "https://charts.redwood.example.com/private"
  artifact_image_pull_secret_name = "redwood-regcred"
}
```

## Changed areas / files
- `modules/private-vpc/variables.tf`: add new variables + validations
- `modules/private-vpc/outputs.tf`: expose effective version/window/artifact settings
- `modules/private-vpc/locals.tf`: compute defaults and normalized values
- `modules/private-vpc/helm-values.tpl`: pass values into chart values for installer/CP gating
- `examples/private-vpc/`: add pinned-version + window example
- `docs/private/upgrade.md`: document recommended upgrade flow, pinning, and window semantics

- chore(private): add version pinning variables + outputs
- feat(private): add upgrade window variables with validation and docs
- feat(private): add artifact source inputs (registry/chart repo)

title: Add Terraform modules for Dedicated burst pools (per region + hardware class)
preview:
Context: Dedicated Burst Capacity needs region/hardware-scoped “burst pools” that can be independently sized and rolled out (e.g., us-east/H100, eu-west/L40S), with clear safety controls and consistent tagging/monitoring. This PR adds reusable Terraform modules + environment wiring to provision burst pools per region/hardware class behind allow-lists, and to standardize alerts/labels used by the admission controller + SRE dashboards.

What’s in this PR: (1) New module: modules/burst_pool (GPU pool + autoscaler + quotas/labels). The module provisions a dedicated node pool / capacity group for “managed burst” with explicit isolation labels/taints so only burst workloads land there. Supports both “shared burst pool” (multi-tenant) and “segmented burst pool” (by tier) via a pool_id + tier label. (2) New module: modules/burst_pool_observability to attach baseline monitors and alert routes (capacity saturation, provisioning failures, node readiness, and a conservative fleet safety cap indicator exported as a metric). (3) Environment wiring: envs/{staging,prod}/{region}/{hw_class}/burst_pools.tf with a standardized map-driven configuration: enablement flag, target_min/target_max, safety_buffer_percent, and rollout_allowlist for region/hw class. (4) Standard tagging and cost allocation: tags include product=dedicated, feature=burst, region, hw_class, pool_id, and owner=fleet-capacity. Adds per-pool cost center tag to match Finance’s reporting needs. (5) Outputs/inputs for downstream: exports pool identifiers and labels required by the admission controller and routing (pool_name, pool_labels, taint_keys, capacity_class). This is intentionally a minimal interface; admission control policy still lives in the control plane.

Guardrails / rollout: Defaults are safe: burst pools are disabled everywhere unless explicitly enabled per env + per region/hw class allowlist. For prod, the initial plan is to enable only us-east/H100 and us-west/H100 in a follow-up apply once sizing is signed off (see ENG-4902). The module includes a hard “max_total_gpus” cap and requires explicit acknowledgement var (ack_burst_pool_cost_risk=true) in prod to prevent accidental large scale-ups.

Testing: CI runs terraform fmt/validate + tflint. This PR also adds a lightweight “plan matrix” job for staging envs (no apply) to ensure module composition is valid across region/hw variants.

Notes / follow-ups: (a) We intentionally do NOT support cross-region bursting here; pools are created and consumed within the same region. (b) We’re not wiring customer-specific pools in this initial pass; segmentation is only by tier labels to support fairness experiments while still being Redwood-managed. (c) We’ll add the first prod enablement + capacity numbers in a separate PR after final sizing review and procurement confirmation.

References: ENG-4902 fleet safety cap + global circuit breaker work; PM-512 design direction for burst pool topology; SRE alerting thresholds to be tuned post dogfood.

title: Add retention policy config resources/modules for Private deployments
preview: Summary: Adds Terraform resources and a small helper module to configure retention controls for Redwood Private deployments where the control plane supports the retention policy service (policy schema v1.1). This is intended to reduce Security/GRC friction during enterprise onboarding by making retention configuration auditable, repeatable, and exportable. Motivation: Several Private design partners requested “infrastructure as code” for retention defaults and per-tenant overrides to align with internal change control. This PR wires up policy CRUD via the retention policy API and provides safe-by-default validation consistent with the control plane schema (min/max TTLs per tier enforced server-side; Terraform additionally validates basic shape for faster feedback). Scope: - New resource: redwood_retention_policy (create/update/delete + read) - New resource: redwood_retention_policy_attachment (attach policy to tenant/logging config where supported) - New data source: redwood_retention_policy (lookup by name/id) - New module: modules/retention_policy_private (opinionated defaults + examples) - Docs: provider docs + examples for common regulated profiles (short request-log retention + longer audit-log retention) Behavior notes: - Private-only: resources require provider config `deployment_mode = "private"` (or equivalent) and will error clearly in Hosted/Dedicated. - Legal hold is intentionally not modeled as a Terraform switch in v1; holds are handled via break-glass workflow and audited operations (see Security guidance). - Deletions are “policy object deletion” only; actual data purge is handled by server-side TTL enforcement jobs. Terraform deletion will remove the policy reference; it does not guarantee immediate physical deletion of already-stored data. - Update semantics: changes produce a new policy revision server-side (change history kept); Terraform diffs only show the desired current config. Example usage: `resource "redwood_retention_policy" "regulated" { name = "regulated-default" data_class { kind = "audit_logs" ttl_days = 365 } data_class { kind = "request_response_logs" ttl_days = 14 } data_class { kind = "traces" ttl_days = 30 } }` and attachment via `redwood_retention_policy_attachment` (tenant_id + policy_id). Testing: - Unit tests for schema normalization + diff suppression for unordered data_class blocks - Acceptance-style tests gated behind `REDWOOD_PRIVATE_ENDPOINT` and `REDWOOD_PRIVATE_TOKEN` (skipped in PR CI, run nightly) - Local verification steps documented in docs. Rollout: No breaking changes to existing provider behavior. Users may optionally adopt the new module; existing logging_config resources remain supported. Follow-ups: - Add import examples for existing policies (tracked separately) - Expand module to cover per-region overrides once control plane enables it for Private (future work).
